title: Topic 238: Why Git Gud Culture Is Toxic: The Psychology of Gatekeeping
content:
# Topic 238: Why Git Gud Culture Is Toxic: The Psychology of Gatekeeping

## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the murky, often-shouted world of "git gud" culture, a phenomenon where seasoned gamers, with the subtlety of a charging rhinoceros, demand that newcomers spontaneously develop god-tier skills or face digital exile. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this gatekeeping behavior, which is less about fostering skill and more about a desperate need to feel special in a world of infinite respawns. Through a rigorous, yet undeniably hilarious, analysis of social identity theory, cognitive dissonance, and the human desire to be the biggest fish in a very specific, pixelated pond, we reveal how "git gud" is not a helpful mantra but a toxic battle cry. This paper serves as a crucial, if not slightly sarcastic, reminder that the point of gaming is, in fact, to have fun—a concept apparently lost on those who believe true joy is found only in the tears of a struggling noob.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand, sprawling coliseum of online gaming, a single, guttural phrase echoes from the lips of self-proclaimed veterans and keyboard warriors alike: "Git gud." This eloquent, two-word decree, a linguistic gem born from the crucible of internet forums and heated in-game chats, is ostensibly a call to self-improvement. However, this paper argues that the "git gud" mentality is not a benign form of tough love but a toxic manifestation of gatekeeping. Through a multidisciplinary lens that combines psychology, sociology, and a healthy dose of comedic observation, we will dissect the motivations behind this digital gatekeeping. We will journey into the minds of those who wield "git gud" as a weapon, examining how concepts like social identity theory and in-group favoritism turn a shared hobby into a battle for dominance. This paper will demonstrate that the true toxicity of "git gud" culture lies not in its demand for skill, but in its insidious message that some people simply do not belong, reminding everyone that video games are supposed to be a source of joy, not a new way to feel bad about yourself.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the matter of people being jerks to each other online. While the specific phrase "git gud" may not grace the hallowed pages of every psychology journal, the underlying behaviors of gatekeeping, in-group bias, and toxicity have been studied with a rigor that would make any gatekeeper proud. This review will take a whimsical tour through the existing literature, connecting the dots between established psychological theories and the bizarre world of gaming culture.

One of the foundational concepts for understanding "git gud" culture is **Social Identity Theory**, a framework that posits people derive a sense of self-esteem from their group memberships [1]. In the context of gaming, this means that being part of an "elite" group of skilled players can be a significant source of personal pride. The theory suggests that to enhance their self-image, individuals will often display favoritism toward their in-group and, more importantly for our purposes, hostility toward the out-group—in this case, the hapless "noobs" [2]. This explains why a seasoned *League of Legends* player might feel a surge of righteous indignation at the sight of a newcomer fumbling with their character's abilities. It's not just about the game; it's about defending the perceived prestige of their digital tribe.

Further reinforcing this is the work on **gatekeeping in virtual communities**. Barzilai-Nahon (2006) noted that gatekeeping is a fundamental aspect of information control, and in online spaces, it becomes a tool for wielding power [3]. While her work was not specifically about gaming, the parallels are undeniable. The "git gud" command is a form of gatekeeping, a test of worthiness. It is a declaration that there is a right way to be a part of the community, and the gatekeeper is the one who decides who is worthy of entry. This is often framed as maintaining standards, but as many a frustrated newcomer can attest, it often feels more like a digital bouncer with an arbitrary dress code.

The concept of **cognitive dissonance** also offers a compelling lens through which to view this phenomenon. This theory suggests that individuals experience discomfort when holding conflicting beliefs or when their actions contradict their beliefs. A gamer who has spent thousands of hours mastering a difficult game may experience dissonance when they see a "casual" player enjoying the same game without the same level of dedication. To resolve this, they might denigrate the casual player's experience, insisting that the *only* valid way to play is their own, highly demanding one. The "git gud" mantra becomes a way to justify their own immense time investment and to alleviate the uncomfortable thought that perhaps, just perhaps, they could have spent those hours doing something else, like learning to bake or talking to their family.

Finally, we must acknowledge the growing body of research on **toxicity in gaming**. Studies have shown that the anonymity and disinhibition of online environments can lead to a significant increase in aggressive and hostile behavior [4]. The "git gud" culture is a prime example of this, a socially acceptable form of toxicity that masquerades as helpful advice. It is a way to be dismissive, condescending, and exclusionary, all under the guise of promoting skill. As we will explore in the following sections, this behavior has real-world consequences, not just for the mental health of the players, but for the consumer behavior that shapes the gaming industry itself.

## ANALYSIS

This section dissects the psychological motivations of the gatekeeping gamer, revealing the human story behind every "git gud" demand.

At the heart of "git gud" culture is a desperate, almost primal, need for **validation**. The gatekeeper, often a player who has invested a significant portion of their life mastering a particular game, has tied their self-worth to their in-game achievements. This is not just a hobby; it is a core part of their identity. When a newcomer arrives, blissfully unaware of the intricate mechanics and unwritten rules of the game, they represent a threat to this carefully constructed identity. The newcomer's lack of skill is not just an inconvenience; it is an invalidation of the gatekeeper's own expertise. By demanding that the newcomer "git gud," the gatekeeper is not just offering advice; they are reaffirming their own superiority and, by extension, their own self-worth. It is the digital equivalent of a self-administered pat on the back, just with more shouting.

This need for validation is often coupled with a healthy dose of **in-group favoritism**. As our literature review suggested, humans are tribal creatures, and the gaming world is no exception. The "elite" players form a tight-knit in-group, and the "noobs" are the ever-present out-group. The "git gud" command is a way of policing the borders of this tribe. It is a way of saying, "You are not one of us, and you will not be until you have proven yourself worthy." This creates a powerful sense of belonging for those on the inside and a formidable barrier for those on the outside. The irony, of course, is that every single member of the in-group was once a noob themselves, a fact they conveniently forget in their quest for exclusivity.

Let us also consider the role of **cognitive dissonance** in this whole charade. Imagine, for a moment, that you have spent 5,000 hours of your life mastering the art of the headshot in *Counter-Strike*. You have sacrificed sleep, social events, and possibly personal hygiene to achieve this level of skill. Then, a new player joins the game, running around with a shotgun, having the time of their life, and occasionally getting a lucky kill. This is a direct challenge to your worldview. If this person can have fun without putting in the same effort as you, what does that say about your own life choices? To resolve this cognitive dissonance, you have two options: either admit that you may have a slight problem with time management, or you can denigrate the new player's experience. The latter is, of course, the easier option. And so, you type those two magical words: "Git gud." The dissonance is resolved, and your life choices are once again validated.

Finally, we cannot ignore the influence of the **Dunning-Kruger effect**, a cognitive bias in which people with low ability at a task overestimate their ability. While this may seem counterintuitive, it can also apply to the gatekeepers. A player who is merely competent, but not truly exceptional, may be the most vocal in their demands for others to "git gud." This is because their own position in the skill hierarchy is precarious. They are not good enough to be universally recognized as elite, but they are just good enough to look down on the true beginners. By gatekeeping, they create a clear distinction between themselves and the noobs, solidifying their own fragile sense of competence. It is a classic case of the middle manager of the gaming world, desperately trying to feel important.

In essence, the "git gud" culture is a complex cocktail of psychological defense mechanisms, tribal instincts, and good old-fashioned insecurity. It is a performance, a way for players to act out a drama of superiority and exclusivity in a world where the stakes are, in the grand scheme of things, incredibly low. And while it may be toxic, it is also, from a safe distance, profoundly entertaining.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The theoretical and psychological frameworks we've discussed are all well and good, but to truly appreciate the majestic absurdity of "git gud" culture, we must venture into the wild and observe it in its natural habitat. The following are but a few select specimens from the vast and colorful menagerie of gaming gatekeeping.

**Exhibit A: The *Dark Souls* "Purists"**

The *Dark Souls* series, a franchise so notoriously difficult it makes tax returns look like a walk in the park, is the quintessential breeding ground for "git gud" enthusiasts. When a journalist famously struggled to play the game, the community's response was not one of empathy or guidance, but a deafening chorus of "git gud." This incident became a case study in gatekeeping, with the "purists" arguing that the game's difficulty is a core part of its artistic vision and that any attempt to make it more accessible would be a betrayal of that vision. The underlying message was clear: if you can't handle the heat, get out of the Blighttown kitchen. The idea that someone might want to experience the game's rich lore and beautiful world without being repeatedly flattened by a giant demon was, to them, anathema.

**Exhibit B: The Fighting Game Community (FGC)**

The FGC is another fascinating case study. With its complex mechanics, demanding execution, and a skill ceiling that extends into the stratosphere, the fighting game genre has a notoriously high barrier to entry. The "git gud" mentality is so ingrained in this community that it is practically a rite of passage. Newcomers are expected to spend countless hours in training mode, learning intricate combos and frame data, before they are even considered worthy of a match. While this dedication to mastery is admirable in its own way, it can also be incredibly alienating. The FGC's gatekeepers, often found lurking in online forums and local tournaments, are quick to dismiss anyone who is not willing to make this significant time investment, effectively ensuring that their community remains a small, insular, and slightly intimidating club.

**Exhibit C: *League of Legends* and the Elo Hell**

No discussion of gaming toxicity would be complete without a mention of *League of Legends*, a game with a reputation for a player base so toxic it could probably melt lead. The game's ranked system, which places players in a hierarchy of skill levels, creates a fertile ground for the "git gud" mentality. Players in the lower ranks, a place affectionately known as "Elo hell," are constantly berated by their more skilled (or at least, more vocal) teammates. Every mistake is met with a barrage of insults and, of course, the inevitable demand to "git gud." This creates a vicious cycle of negativity, where players are too afraid to learn and improve for fear of being verbally assaulted. It is a perfect example of how the "git gud" culture, far from promoting skill, can actually stifle it.

These examples, and countless others like them, illustrate the real-world impact of the "git gud" culture. It is a force that can turn a shared passion into a source of anxiety, frustration, and exclusion. And, as we will see in the next section, it has significant implications for how we understand not just gaming, but human behavior and consumer habits as a whole.

## DISCUSSION

The "git gud" culture in gaming offers a window into broader patterns of human behavior and consumer habits. Gaming communities are microcosms of society, and by examining this gatekeeping phenomenon, we can gain insights into our own behavior.

From a consumer behavior perspective, the "git gud" culture has a fascinatingly paradoxical effect. On one hand, it can be a powerful driver of engagement and spending. The desire to "git gud" and gain acceptance into an elite group can motivate players to invest not just time, but also money. They might buy cosmetic items to signal their veteran status, purchase strategy guides or coaching sessions, or even upgrade their hardware in the hopes of gaining a competitive edge. In this sense, the gatekeepers, in their own toxic way, are acting as unwitting brand evangelists, reinforcing the value of the game and encouraging deeper investment from those who are desperate to belong.

On the other hand, and this is a much larger hand, this toxicity is incredibly damaging to the long-term health of the gaming market. For every player who is motivated by the "git gud" challenge, there are likely several others who are driven away. The gaming industry is constantly seeking to expand its audience, to bring in new players from all walks of life. The "git gud" culture is a direct impediment to this goal. It creates a hostile environment for newcomers, particularly those from underrepresented groups, and can lead to high churn rates. A player who is constantly told they are not good enough is unlikely to stick around and spend money on the game. They will simply take their leisure time and their disposable income elsewhere, to a more welcoming community. This is the great irony of the gatekeeper: in their attempt to protect the purity of their hobby, they are actively shrinking its potential market.

Ultimately, the "git gud" culture is a powerful reminder that consumption is not just about the product itself; it is about the community and the identity that surrounds it. People do not just buy a game; they buy into a culture. And if that culture is toxic, exclusionary, and just plain mean, it doesn't matter how good the game is. The modern consumer, particularly in the digital age, is looking for more than just a product; they are looking for a place to belong. And the most successful brands, in gaming and beyond, will be those that understand that building a welcoming community is just as important as building a great product.

## CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the clarion call to "git gud" is far more than a simple, if grammatically challenged, piece of advice. It is a psychological defense mechanism, a tribal war cry, and a desperate plea for validation all rolled into one. We have seen how this phenomenon, rooted in social identity theory, cognitive dissonance, and a dash of the Dunning-Kruger effect, serves to protect the fragile egos of a self-appointed elite. While it may provide a fleeting sense of superiority for the gatekeepers, its long-term effects are undeniably toxic, creating a hostile environment that stifles growth, alienates newcomers, and ultimately harms the very communities it purports to protect. Perhaps, then, the most skillful move in the game of life is not to "git gud," but to "git kinder." Or, at the very least, to recognize that the ultimate goal of gaming is not to be the best, but to have the most fun—a revolutionary concept that might just save us all from the tyranny of the perpetually unimpressed.

## REFERENCES

[1] Tajfel, H., & Turner, J. C. (1979). An integrative theory of intergroup conflict. In W. G. Austin & S. Worchel (Eds.), *The social psychology of intergroup relations* (pp. 33-47). Brooks/Cole.

[2] Brewer, M. B. (1999). The psychology of prejudice: Ingroup love or outgroup hate?. *Journal of Social Issues*, 55(3), 429-444.

[3] Barzilai-Nahon, K. (2006). Gatekeepers, virtual communities and the gated: Multidimensional tensions in cyberspace. *International Journal of Communications Law & Policy*, 11.

[4] Suler, J. (2004). The online disinhibition effect. *CyberPsychology & Behavior*, 7(3), 321-326.
